# Dissimilarity of thermochronology cooling paths

Calculates the dissimilarity matrix and Hopkins statistic for
thermochronology cooling paths using the *Hausdorff* or the *Fréchet
distance*.

## Usage

``` r
path_diss(x, dist = c("Hausdorff", "Frechet"), densify = 0, simplify = 0, ...)

path_diss_parallel(x, simplify = 0, ...)
```

## Arguments

- x:

  either an object of class `"HeFTy"` (output of
  [`read_hefty()`](https://tobiste.github.io/thermoclustr/reference/read_hefty.md))
  or a `data.frame` containing the `time`, `temperature`, and `segment`
  columns of the modeled paths.

- dist:

  character. Algorithm to calculate a dissimilarity matrix (distance)
  for lines; either `Hausdorff` (the default) or `Frechet`.

- densify:

  numeric. optionally use a value between 0 and 1 to densify the
  geometry description. Default is 0.

- simplify:

  numeric. Optional tolerance parameter to specify the amount of
  simplification of path geometries in order to save processing time.
  The tolerance is in the same unit as temperature and should relate to
  the expected temperature spread. The simplification is applied before
  the densification. Some good values are 0.5, 2.5, 5, and 10. Default
  is 0 (no simplification).

- ...:

  (optional) arguments passed to
  [`cluster_tendency()`](https://tobiste.github.io/thermoclustr/reference/cluster_tendency.md).

## Value

`tTdiss` object, i.e. a list containing

- `paths`:

  t-T paths as `sf` object in time-temperature space

- `diss`:

  the \\n \times n\\ dissimilarity matrix (\\n\\ is number of paths)

- `method`:

  the dissimilarity algorithm used

- `hopkins`:

  the Hopkins statistic and its p-value.

- `mds`:

  the multidimensional scaling coordinates of the dissimilarity matrix

## Details

The Hausdorff distance is the greatest of all the distances from a point
in one set to the closest point in the other set. The Fréchet distance
additionally takes into account the location and ordering of the points
along the curves (the "flow").

## Note

The algorithm calculates the pairwise dissimilarities between the n
paths and creates a n x n matrix. A large number of paths may end up in
very long processing times and can cause memory issues. Thus, it is
recommended to filter the paths beforehand using either
[`crop_paths()`](https://tobiste.github.io/thermoclustr/reference/crop_paths.md)
or [`subset()`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/subset.html) to, for example,
include only paths with large GOF (see examples), or create a random
subset using, e.g., [`sample()`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/sample.html)
(see examples). Additionally, the `simplify` parameter can be adjusted
to reduce the number of vertices along the paths to speed up the
processing time.

## Examples

``` r
data(tT_paths)
tT_paths_subset <- subset(tT_paths$paths, Comp_GOF >= 0.4)

# calculate the dissimilarities of the paths:
tT_diss <- path_diss(tT_paths_subset, densify = 1)

# the `diss` object of the output can be used for clustering using any
# available cluster algorithm, e.g.:
stats::kmeans(tT_diss$diss, centers = 3)

# using a random subset of paths (in case of too large data)
set.seed(20250411)

## select 100 random path segments:
random_segments <- sample(unique(tT_paths$paths$segment), size = 100)
tT_paths_rnd <- subset(tT_paths$paths, segment %in% random_segments)

path_diss(tT_paths_rnd)
```
